question_title: According to Copernicus, how will March 2026 rank globally among the warmest Marches on record?
summary: March 2026 will most likely rank 4th-5th warmest March globally according to Copernicus, consistent with the persistent pattern of Dec 2025 through Feb 2026 all ranking 5th warmest. The La Niña-to-neutral ENSO transition means 2026 months are substantially cooler than 2024's El Niño-driven records (March 2024 was warmest, March 2025 was 2nd). ERA5 analysis suggests March 2026 is approximately 1°C below March 2024 and 2025 globally, making warmest or 2nd-3rd rankings essentially impossible. However, extraordinary regional heat events (warmest US March on record, warmest Moscow March, record heat in Northern Europe/Africa/Australia) and rising ocean temperatures (near record calendar-day levels per late March reports) could push the ranking slightly higher than the Jan/Feb pattern. The Copernicus March 2026 bulletin is not yet published (expected ~April 7-10).

factors.description: ERA5 full-month data analysis from Q42813 prediction history: March 2026 avg ~1.1°C below March 2024 record and ~1.0°C below March 2025. Even the warmest day of March 2026 was reportedly cooler than the coolest day of March 2024 globally.
factors.conditional: If the ERA5 data analysis is accurate and representative of the full month

factors.description: Jan 2026 and Feb 2026 both ranked 5th warmest globally per Copernicus. Dec 2025 was also 5th warmest. Establishes consistent pattern during La Niña/neutral ENSO conditions.

ENSO: La Niña to neutral transition in early 2026 vs strong El Niño in 2024. This is the dominant factor explaining why 2026 months are substantially cooler than 2024. El Niño emergence not expected until summer 2026.

Extraordinary regional heat in March 2026: warmest US March on record (4000+ daily records broken, 16 states set all-time March records), warmest Moscow March, record heat in Northern Europe, Africa, Australia. Could push global average slightly higher than Jan/Feb rankings.

Former CWA director noted March 28 that ocean temperatures reached highest levels in modern analysis era (calendar-day basis). Rising SSTs and early El Niño signals. Feb 2026 SST was joint 2nd highest for February. Could boost March 2026 global average toward higher ranking.
factors.conditional: If late-March ocean warming significantly raises the monthly average

factors.description: The specific ERA5 ~12.955°C figure for March 2026 implies a negative anomaly vs 1991-2020 average, which conflicts with the 5th-warmest pattern of recent months and extensive regional heat. Downweighting this specific number while retaining the directional signal that March 2026 is well below 2024/2025.

reasoning:
**Forecast: March 2026 Global Temperature Ranking (Copernicus)**

| Outcome | Probability |
|---------|------------|
| Warmest on record | 2% |
| 2nd-3rd warmest | 12% |
| **4th-5th warmest** | **55%** |
| Lower than 5th | 31% |

**Key reasoning:**

The strongest signal is the consistent pattern of Dec 2025 through Feb 2026 all ranking 5th warmest globally according to Copernicus, driven by the La Niña-to-neutral ENSO transition (vs strong El Niño in 2024). ERA5 data analysis shows March 2026 is ~1°C below March 2024 and 2025 at the global level, making the top ranks essentially impossible.

The extraordinary regional heat (warmest US March ever, record Moscow/Europe/Africa heat) and rising ocean temperatures could push March 2026 slightly higher than the Jan/Feb pattern, but regional land heat covers too small a fraction of global surface area to materially close the gap with 2024/2025. The most likely outcome is a ranking around 4th-5th warmest, with meaningful probability of 6th or lower if global SSTs didn't rise enough to offset the ENSO cooling.

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the option Warmest on record if Copernicus reports March 2026 as the warmest March globally on record. This question resolves to the option 2nd–3rd warmest if Copernicus reports or clearly implies that March 2026 was the second- or third-warmest March globally. Et cetera. The primary source is the Copernicus Climate Change Service climate bulletin page at https://climate.copernicus.eu/climate-bulletin
fine_print: Use Copernicus’s first bulletin covering March 2026. If Copernicus provides temperature values but not an explicit rank, infer the option from Copernicus’s own comparison wording on the relevant bulletin page. Candidate Sources: Copernicus March temperature page when posted under the same domain
